title: Capacitor Size Calculator
tagline: Calculate the physical dimensions, energy storage, and power density of a parallel-plate capacitor from its capacitance, voltage, and dielectric properties.
intro: Enter the required capacitance, operating voltage, dielectric constant, and dielectric strength to calculate plate dimensions, energy storage, and power density.

error.invalid: Please enter valid positive numbers in all fields.

examples.heading: Worked Examples
examples.intro: Click an example to load it into the calculator.

items.input: C = 1 μF, V = 12 V, εᵣ = 1 (air), DS = 3 MV/m
items.output: Area ≈ 0.452 m², Energy = 72 μJ, Power density ≈ 39.8 J/m³
items.note: Simple air-dielectric capacitor for basic electronics; large plate area required due to εᵣ = 1.

items.input: C = 10 μF, V = 1000 V, εᵣ = 8 (ceramic), DS = 8 MV/m
items.output: d = 0.125 mm, Area ≈ 17.65 m², Energy = 5 J, Power density ≈ 2.27 kJ/m³
items.note: High-voltage ceramic capacitor; even with εᵣ = 8 the large capacitance requires substantial plate area.

items.input: C = 100 mF, V = 50 V, εᵣ = 2.2 (polymer), DS = 5 MV/m
items.output: d = 10 μm, Area ≈ 51,337 m², Energy = 125 J, Power density ≈ 243.5 J/m³
items.note: 100 mF at 50 V requires enormous plate area — illustrating why electrolytic designs are preferred for large capacitance.

items.input: C = 0.1 μF, V = 5 V, εᵣ = 100 (ceramic), DS = 2 MV/m
items.output: d = 2.5 μm, Area ≈ 2.82×10⁻⁴ m², Energy = 1.25 μJ, Power density ≈ 1.77 kJ/m³
items.note: Miniature high-εᵣ ceramic capacitor; high dielectric constant yields very compact dimensions.

about.heading: About the Capacitor Size Calculator
about.body:
A capacitor is a fundamental passive electronic component that stores electrical energy in an electric field between two conductive plates separated by an insulating material called the dielectric. The capacitor size calculator helps engineers, students, and hobbyists determine the physical dimensions of a parallel-plate capacitor given its electrical specifications.

The core governing equation is the parallel-plate capacitance formula: C = ε₀ × εᵣ × A / d, where C is the capacitance in farads, ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m is the permittivity of free space, εᵣ is the relative permittivity (dielectric constant) of the insulating material, A is the plate area in square metres, and d is the separation between the plates in metres. Rearranging, the required plate area is A = C × d / (ε₀ × εᵣ).

The plate separation d is determined by the operating voltage and the dielectric strength of the material. The dielectric strength is the maximum electric field a material can withstand before breaking down, measured in volts per metre. Setting d = V / dielectricStrength gives the minimum thickness needed to avoid breakdown at the specified voltage. In practice, engineers add a safety margin—typically the operating voltage should be 50 % or less of the rated breakdown voltage.

Once plate area and thickness are known, other important parameters follow directly. The energy stored in a capacitor is E = ½ CV², showing that energy scales with the square of voltage—doubling voltage quadruples energy storage. The dielectric volume is Vol = A × d, and the volumetric energy density (power density) is E / Vol = ½ ε₀ εᵣ Eₘₐₓ², where Eₘₐₓ is the electric field strength. Maximising the dielectric constant and operating close to (but below) the breakdown field maximises energy density.

The choice of dielectric material is the single biggest lever for miniaturisation. Air has εᵣ = 1 and dielectric strength ≈ 3 MV/m. Polypropylene film (εᵣ ≈ 2.2, DS ≈ 600 MV/m) offers excellent high-frequency performance. Ceramic materials range from εᵣ ≈ 8 (Class I, stable) to εᵣ > 10,000 (Class II/III, temperature-dependent). Electrolytic capacitors use a microscopic oxide layer as the dielectric, achieving very high capacitance in a small volume but limited to one-polarity applications.

This calculator models the ideal parallel-plate geometry. Real capacitors differ due to edge effects (fringing fields near the plate perimeter), wound or stacked construction, parasitic resistance and inductance, temperature coefficients of the dielectric, and ageing. For precise design work, always consult the manufacturer's datasheet and apply appropriate safety derating—typically operate at no more than 60–70 % of the rated voltage and within the specified temperature range.

howto.heading: How to Use the Capacitor Size Calculator
steps: Enter the required capacitance in farads. For microfarad values, use scientific notation (e.g. 1 μF = 1e-6) or the decimal equivalent (0.000001).
Enter the operating voltage in volts. This is the DC voltage the capacitor will see in the circuit. For AC circuits, use the peak voltage.
Enter the dielectric constant (εᵣ) of your chosen material. Air ≈ 1, polypropylene ≈ 2.2, polyester ≈ 3.2, ceramic ≈ 8–10,000.
Enter the dielectric strength in V/m. Air ≈ 3×10⁶, polypropylene ≈ 600×10⁶, ceramic ≈ 8×10⁶. This determines the minimum plate separation for safe operation.

Click Calculate. The results show plate distance, plate area, energy stored, dielectric volume, power density, and electric field at operating voltage. Use the example buttons to load typical capacitor configurations.

items.q: Why is the calculated plate area so large for air capacitors?
items.a: Air has a dielectric constant of only 1 and a relatively low dielectric strength (~3 MV/m). Because capacitance is proportional to εᵣ × A / d, achieving large capacitance with εᵣ = 1 requires a very large plate area. That is why practical capacitors use high-εᵣ materials such as ceramics: a dielectric constant of 1000 reduces the required plate area by a factor of 1000.

items.q: What is dielectric strength and why does it matter?
items.a: Dielectric strength is the maximum electric field (V/m) a material can withstand before the insulator breaks down and current flows through it, permanently damaging the capacitor. It determines the minimum plate separation d = V / DS for a given operating voltage. Higher dielectric strength allows thinner dielectrics, which increases capacitance (since C ∝ 1/d) and reduces physical size for the same capacitance and voltage rating.

items.q: How does energy density depend on dielectric properties?
items.a: The volumetric energy density is ½ × ε₀ × εᵣ × E², where E is the electric field. To maximise energy density you want both a high dielectric constant and operation close to the breakdown field. However, high-εᵣ materials often have lower dielectric strength, so the optimal material balances these two properties. Polypropylene film, for example, has a modest εᵣ ≈ 2.2 but an extremely high dielectric strength of ~600 MV/m, making it excellent for high-energy-density applications.

items.q: What safety margin should I apply to the calculated plate distance?
items.a: Most capacitor manufacturers rate their components at a voltage that gives a safety factor of at least 1.5–2× over the expected breakdown voltage. In circuit design, it is good practice to operate capacitors at no more than 60–70 % of their rated voltage. For this calculator, the computed plate distance assumes operation exactly at the dielectric strength limit—apply at least a 2× safety factor on plate distance (or equivalently, halve the effective voltage rating) for reliable long-term operation.

items.q: Does this calculator work for cylindrical or wound capacitors?
items.a: This calculator models the ideal parallel-plate geometry. Cylindrical (rolled) capacitors used in electrolytics and film capacitors have the same fundamental formula for a thin strip rolled into a cylinder, so the computed plate area is directly applicable—it is the total active area of the foil strip. Edge effects, lead inductance, and equivalent series resistance are not modelled and become important at high frequencies.

items.q: How do I compare capacitors with different dielectrics for the same application?
items.a: Fix the required capacitance and operating voltage, then vary the dielectric constant and dielectric strength for different materials. The calculator will show plate area, volume, and energy density for each material. A smaller volume at the same energy means better efficiency. Also consider temperature stability, frequency response, and cost: ceramic Class I (NP0/C0G) is very stable but limited to small values, while Class II (X7R, X5R) offers high capacitance density with some voltage and temperature dependence.
